Press enter or click to view image in full size

When you’re writing test automation with Playwright, Cypress, Selenium, or another JavaScript-based framework, data types matter more than they initially appear.
A price displayed on a web page may look like a number to a human, but JavaScript may receive it as a string. A missing API property may return undefined, while an intentionally empty field may return null. If your automation code does not understand these differences, you can end up with failed assertions, incorrect calculations, or even false positives.
This article covers the core JavaScript primitive data types commonly encountered in QA automation and, more importantly, how to handle one of the most common testing problems: converting UI text into numbers correctly.

The 5 Core Data Types Commonly Used in QA Automation
JavaScript has more primitive data types than the five covered here, including bigint and symbol. However, the following five are the ones QA engineers encounter most frequently in everyday test automation.
Data TypeWhat It RepresentsExample in TestingStringText dataLabels, error messages, product namesNumberIntegers and decimal valuesPrices, quantities, element countsBooleantrue or falseVisibility, enabled state, checkbox statenullAn intentionally empty valueAn API field explicitly set to no valueundefinedA value that has not been assigned or a missing propertyA missing property in test configuration
Let’s look at each one from a QA automation perspective.

String
A string represents text.
Strings can be created using:
const productName = "Laptop";
const errorMessage = 'Invalid password';
const statusMessage = `Order completed successfully`;In QA automation, strings are everywhere.
Common examples include:

• Button labels

• Page titles

• Error messages

• Product names

• URLs

• API response values

• Input field values

• Test IDs and selectors
Example:
const actualMessage = await page
.getByTestId('error-message')
.innerText();expect(actualMessage).toBe("Invalid username or password");The text returned from the UI is a string.

Number
The number data type represents both integers and decimal values in JavaScript.
const itemCount = 5;
const productPrice = 45.99;
const timeout = 5000;In QA automation, numbers are commonly used for:

• Product prices

• Item quantities

• Element counts

• Timeouts

• Response times

• Pagination values

• Calculated totals
Example:
const productCount = await page
.locator('.product-card')
.count();expect(productCount).toBe(10);The value returned by .count() is a number.

Boolean
A Boolean can have only two values:
true
falseBooleans are extremely common in test automation because many validations represent yes-or-no conditions.
For example:
const isVisible = await loginButton.isVisible();
const isEnabled = await submitButton.isEnabled();
const isChecked = await rememberMeCheckbox.isChecked();Each variable contains either true or false.
You can then assert the result:
expect(isVisible).toBe(true);
expect(isEnabled).toBe(true);
expect(isChecked).toBe(false);In Playwright, you can often use web-first assertions directly:
await expect(loginButton).toBeVisible();
await expect(submitButton).toBeEnabled();
await expect(rememberMeCheckbox).not.toBeChecked();This is generally preferable for UI testing because Playwright’s web-first assertions automatically wait for the expected condition.

null
null represents an intentionally empty value.
It usually means:
A value is expected to exist conceptually, but currently no value has been assigned intentionally.
Example API response:
const response = {
orderId: 12345,
processedAt: null
};Here, processedAt exists as a property, but its value is intentionally empty.
This could mean that the order has not yet been processed.
You can validate it like this:
expect(response.processedAt).toBeNull();
undefined
undefined usually means that a variable has not been assigned a value or that a requested property does not exist.
Example:
let environment;console.log(environment); // undefinedAnother common QA scenario is accessing a missing configuration property:
const testConfig = {
browser: "chromium"
};console.log(testConfig.baseUrl); // undefinedThe baseUrl property does not exist, so JavaScript returns undefined.
You can validate this using:
expect(testConfig.baseUrl).toBeUndefined();
null vs undefined: A Common QA Confusion
These two values may look similar, but they represent different situations.
const apiResponse = {
middleName: null
};console.log(apiResponse.middleName); // null
console.log(apiResponse.age); // undefinedThe difference is important:

• middleName exists and was intentionally assigned an empty value.

• age does not exist at all.
This distinction matters when validating API contracts.
For example, these two API responses are not necessarily equivalent:
{
"middleName": null
}and:
{}Depending on the API specification, one may be valid while the other may indicate a defect.

The QA Trap: UI Text Is Usually a String
One of the most common mistakes in UI automation happens when reading numeric-looking values from a web page.
Suppose the UI displays:
45You may think you’re working with the number 45.
But if you retrieve that value using methods such as:
await locator.innerText();
await locator.textContent();
await input.getAttribute('value');the result is generally a string.
Consider this example:
const itemPrice1 = "45";
const itemPrice2 = "5";const total = itemPrice1 + itemPrice2;console.log(total);You might expect:
50But JavaScript produces:
455Why?
Because both values are strings.
The + operator performs string concatenation instead of mathematical addition.
"45" + "5"becomes:
"455"This can create serious problems in automation if the test does not explicitly convert UI values before performing calculations.

A Realistic UI Automation Example
Suppose a shopping cart displays:
Item Price: $45.99
Shipping: $5.00
Total: $50.99Your automation test needs to verify that:
Item Price + Shipping = TotalThe first step may look like this:
const uiPrice = await page
.getByTestId('item-price')
.innerText();const uiShipping = await page
.getByTestId('shipping-cost')
.innerText();const uiTotal = await page
.getByTestId('total-price')
.innerText();The values might be:
uiPrice = "$45.99";
uiShipping = "$5.00";
uiTotal = "$50.99";These are all strings.
Before performing arithmetic, they must be converted into numbers.

Converting Strings to Numbers in JavaScript
The three commonly used conversion methods are:

• Number()

• parseInt()

• parseFloat()
Each behaves differently.

Using Number()
Number() converts a value into a number.
Number("45"); // 45
Number("45.99"); // 45.99It is strict about the entire string being a valid numeric value.
Number("$45"); // NaN
Number("45px"); // NaN
Number("45 items"); // NaNNaN means:
Not a NumberThis makes Number() useful when you expect the entire string to contain only a valid number.
Example:
const quantityText = "5";const quantity = Number(quantityText);expect(quantity).toBe(5);
Using parseInt()
parseInt() converts the beginning of a string into an integer.
parseInt("45", 10); // 45
parseInt("45px", 10); // 45
parseInt("45.99", 10); // 45The second argument is the radix.
10means decimal, or base 10.
A clear and explicit pattern is:
parseInt(value, 10);Use parseInt() when you need a whole number.
Common QA examples include:

• Product quantities

• Pagination numbers

• Element counts represented as text

• Pixel values

• Percentage values when decimals are irrelevant
Example:
const quantityText = "5 items";const quantity = parseInt(quantityText, 10);console.log(quantity); // 5However, parseInt() only parses from the beginning of the string.
parseInt("$45", 10); // NaNSince the string begins with $, parsing cannot start.

Using parseFloat()
parseFloat() converts the beginning of a string into a decimal number.
parseFloat("45.99"); // 45.99
parseFloat("45.99 items"); // 45.99It preserves decimal values.
Compare:
parseInt("45.99", 10); // 45
parseFloat("45.99"); // 45.99However, like parseInt(), parsing must begin with a valid numeric character.
parseFloat("$45.99"); // NaNThe $ symbol appears before the number, so the string must first be cleaned.

Step-by-Step QA Application: E-Commerce Price Validation
Imagine you’re testing a shopping cart.
The UI displays:
Item Price: $45.99
Shipping: $5.00
Total: $50.99Your test needs to verify that the displayed total is mathematically correct.
Step 1: Extract the Text from the UI
const uiPrice = "$45.99";
const uiShipping = "$5.00";
const uiTotal = "$50.99";In a real Playwright test, these values might come from:
const uiPrice = await page
.getByTestId('item-price')
.innerText();const uiShipping = await page
.getByTestId('shipping-cost')
.innerText();const uiTotal = await page
.getByTestId('total-price')
.innerText();At this stage, all three values are strings.

Step 2: Sanitize the Strings
Remove the currency symbol before conversion.
const sanitizedPrice = uiPrice.replace('$', '');
const sanitizedShipping = uiShipping.replace('$', '');
const sanitizedTotal = uiTotal.replace('$', '');The resulting values are:
"45.99"
"5.00"
"50.99"They are still strings, but they now contain only numeric characters and decimal points.

Step 3: Convert the Strings to Numbers
Use parseFloat():
const price = parseFloat(sanitizedPrice);
const shipping = parseFloat(sanitizedShipping);
const displayedTotal = parseFloat(sanitizedTotal);Now the values are numbers:
price = 45.99;
shipping = 5;
displayedTotal = 50.99;
Step 4: Calculate the Expected Total
const calculatedTotal = price + shipping;The result is:
50.99
Step 5: Assert the Calculation
Instead of hardcoding the expected value:
expect(calculatedTotal).toBe(50.99);a stronger test compares the calculated total against the total actually displayed by the application:
expect(displayedTotal).toBe(calculatedTotal);This validates the application’s calculation dynamically.
The complete example becomes:
const uiPrice = await page
.getByTestId('item-price')
.innerText();const uiShipping = await page
.getByTestId('shipping-cost')
.innerText();const uiTotal = await page
.getByTestId('total-price')
.innerText();const price = parseFloat(uiPrice.replace('$', ''));
const shipping = parseFloat(uiShipping.replace('$', ''));
const displayedTotal = parseFloat(uiTotal.replace('$', ''));const calculatedTotal = price + shipping;expect(displayedTotal).toBe(calculatedTotal);This is much closer to a real e-commerce validation.

Handling Currency with Commas
Real applications often display values like:
$1,299.99Simply removing $ gives:
1,299.99But:
Number("1,299.99");returns:
NaNThe comma must also be removed.
const uiPrice = "$1,299.99";const sanitizedPrice = uiPrice
.replace('$', '')
.replace(',', '');const price = Number(sanitizedPrice);console.log(price); // 1299.99For larger values containing multiple commas, use a regular expression:
const sanitizedPrice = uiPrice
.replace('$', '')
.replace(/,/g, '');Now every comma is removed.

A Reusable Currency Conversion Function
If your automation framework performs price validation frequently, repeating conversion logic in every test creates unnecessary duplication.
Create a reusable helper instead.
function parseCurrency(value) {
return Number(
value
.replace('$', '')
.replace(/,/g, '')
.trim()
);
}Usage:
const price = parseCurrency("$1,299.99");
const shipping = parseCurrency("$5.00");console.log(price); // 1299.99
console.log(shipping); // 5Your test becomes cleaner:
const price = parseCurrency(uiPrice);
const shipping = parseCurrency(uiShipping);
const displayedTotal = parseCurrency(uiTotal);const calculatedTotal = price + shipping;expect(displayedTotal).toBe(calculatedTotal);This approach improves:

• Readability

• Reusability

• Maintainability

• Consistency across tests

The NaN Trap
When conversion fails, JavaScript may return NaN.
Example:
const price = Number("$45.99");console.log(price); // NaNA dangerous m
